coredata.dc:title: Enhancing efficiency of MAPbI3 perovskite solar cells with carbon quantum dot implanted g-C3N4: Controllable crystallization and surface passivation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Fei
Chen, Weijun
Li, Ye
Shao, Long
He, Li
coredata.dc:description: Organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite films with excellent surface morphology and large grain sizes are crucial for achieving high-performance perovskite solar cells (PSCs). To reduce production costs and meet the growing demand for lightweight thin solar cells, developing ultra-thin absorber layers is an effective approach. However, defects and trap sites are inevitably formed on the surfaces of solution-processed perovskite films. Herein, we present an innovative method that directly incorporates graphite carbon nitride/carbon quantum dots (g-C3N4/CQDs) into the perovskite precursor solution, using them as structural template to modulate film growth. The g-C3N4/CQDs delay crystallization through the formation of a MA·g-C3N4/CQDs intermediate adduct, which reduces light absorption loss by increasing enlarge grain size and minimizing numerous pinhole defects in thin perovskite films. Additionally, the amide bond formed between g-C3N4 and CQDs can accelerate charge transport, as demonstrated by density functional theory calculation. As a result, the PSCs based on high-quality 360 nm MAPbI3:g-C3N4/CQDs films achieved a champion power conversion efficiency of 20.49 %. The devices retained more than 90 % of their initial efficiency for over 500 h in air without encapsulation. This work not only provides valuable insights into the role of 2D/0D carbon-based heterojunctions in PSCs, but also provides a straightforward technique for crystallization regulation to achieve efficient and stable PSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perovskite solar cells
Ultra-thin absorber layer
Carbon-based heterojunction
Power conversion efficiency
Charge transport

U2141239), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 62305045).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of preparation process of CN/CQDs. (b) SEM image of CN/CQDs. (c) TEM image of CN/CQDs. (d) Local enlarged CQDs of CN/CQDs. (e) HRTEM image of CQDs.

(a) XRD patterns of MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films. Top-view SEM images of (b) MAPbI3, (c) MAPbI3:g-C3N4, and (d) MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films. (e) Photographs of MAPbI3 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films during annealing and crystallization at 100°C.
(a) Liquid-state 1H NMR spectra of MAPbI3 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs precursor solutions in DMF-d7. (b) Magnification of the scale between 7.3 and 7.8 ppm. (c) Schematic diagram of the nucleation process.

(a) Schematic diagram of the structure of the PSCs. The cross-section SEM of the (b) MAPbI3:CN/CQDs and (c) MAPbI3 based PSCs. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films. (e) Energy bandgap diagrams of the PSCs.

(a) Current density-voltage characteristics of PSCs based on MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films. (b) EQE curves and J int for PSCs based on MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films. (c) The stabilized maximum power output for MAPbI3 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs devices.

(a) Steady state PL spectra and (b) TRPL spectra of MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs film. (c) EIS of MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs-based devices under dark condition. The inset shows the equivalent circuit. (d) The SCLC curves of MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs based devices. The inset image shows the device configuration.

(a) Survey and (b) high-resolution C 1 s core level XPS spectra of g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs. (c) High-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra of g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs. (d) Ratio of X1/(X2+X3) for the relative contents for diverse types of N 1 s.

(a) Long time stability of devices with and without CN/CQDs in an ambient environment (∼40 % humidity, T = 25°C). (b) Temperature-dependent thermal stability of devices based on MAPbI3 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs. (c) Time-dependent thermal stability of PSCs with and without CN/CQDs annealed at 150°C.
Photovoltaic performance of the PSCs based on different CN/CQDs concentration.
A summary of recent application of carbon nitride compound in PSCs.

head.ce:title: Enhancing efficiency of MAPbI3 perovskite solar cells with carbon quantum dot implanted g-C3N4: Controllable crystallization and surface passivation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Changchun University of Technology, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Changchun University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite films with excellent surface morphology and large grain sizes are crucial for achieving high-performance perovskite solar cells (PSCs). To reduce production costs and meet the growing demand for lightweight thin solar cells, developing ultra-thin absorber layers is an effective approach. However, defects and trap sites are inevitably formed on the surfaces of solution-processed perovskite films. Herein, we present an innovative method that directly incorporates graphite carbon nitride/carbon quantum dots (g-C3N4/CQDs) into the perovskite precursor solution, using them as structural template to modulate film growth. The g-C3N4/CQDs delay crystallization through the formation of a MA·g-C3N4/CQDs intermediate adduct, which reduces light absorption loss by increasing enlarge grain size and minimizing numerous pinhole defects in thin perovskite films. Additionally, the amide bond formed between g-C3N4 and CQDs can accelerate charge transport, as demonstrated by density functional theory calculation. As a result, the PSCs based on high-quality 360 nm MAPbI3:g-C3N4/CQDs films achieved a champion power conversion efficiency of 20.49 %. The devices retained more than 90 % of their initial efficiency for over 500 h in air without encapsulation. This work not only provides valuable insights into the role of 2D/0D carbon-based heterojunctions in PSCs, but also provides a straightforward technique for crystallization regulation to achieve efficient and stable PSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are regarded as the most promising technology to replace silicon-based solar cells due to their excellent photoelectric conversion performance and straightforward manufacturing process [1–6]. Over the past decade, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) increased from 3.8 % to a record value of 26.7 %. This impressive efficiency is primarily attributed to the advantages of perovskite materials, such as their high absorption coefficient, long charge diffusion length, and bipolar transport properties [7–9].

For planar structure PSCs, adequate light absorption by the perovskite light-absorbing layer is crucial for achieving excellent photovoltaic performance and stable power output [10–14]. Generally, perovskite films in PSCs must maintain sufficient thickness to avoid the formation of pinhole defects. However, the limited charge diffusion length poses a significant drawback for thicker films. Given the growing demand for transparent cells in tandem solar cell constructions, developing novel fabrication techniques to produce high-quality thin perovskite films (＜400 nm) with high PCE is essential for advancing the practical application of PSCs. Thin perovskite films can reduce the carrier diffusion distance and accelerate the charge transport. Several recent studies have focused on enhancing the quality of thin perovskite films to boost higher efficient of PSCs [15–17]. For example, Grätzel and co-workers demonstrated that the oxidation-stable rubidium cation could be inset into a “cation cascade” to produce perovskite films thinner than 400 nm with excellent stability [18]. Guo et al. showed that MAPbI3 with tunable thickness could be achieved using a two-substrate confined antisolvent vapor-assisted crystallization method. Solar cells based on 300-nm-thick MAPbI3 exhibited an improved PCE of 20.1 %, which is attributed to the large grain sizes that effectively suppress non-radiative recombination losses and enhance the charge transfer properties [19]. Kaltenbrunner and co-workers reported planar perovskite solar arrays with perovskite film thicknesses of 250–300 nm, achieving a satisfactory PCE and stable operation over several days under normal environmental conditions. The use of a transparent polymer electrode treated with DMSO as the bottom layer enabled the deposition of pinhole-free perovskite films at low temperatures [20]. However, the photovoltaic performance of the PSCs described above has not seen significant improvements. One possible reason is that ultra-thin perovskite films may result in energy loss, it is necessary to regulate crystallization and enhance the grain size to achieve high PCE. In general, perovskite crystallization is a gradual process, which starts from a slow nucleation procedure to a rapid growth process [21,22]. Methods for modifying the nucleation and growth process method include temperature-solvent engineering, multi-dimensional crystal growth, and the construction of growth skeletons [23]. Prefabricating a growth skeleton to provide sufficient space for perovskite growth is one of the most effective and simple approaches. Using highly conductive materials as growth skeletons can accelerate charge extraction and transport.

Recently, carbon-based polymers and carbon nanotubes have been introduced into PSCs as growth templates. For example, Jeon et al. incorporated single-walled carbon nanotubes into the MAPbI3 precursor solution [24]. The single-walled carbon nanotubes acted as crystal growth templates, which effectively passivate the defects in the perovskite film and enhance the light harvesting in planar PSCs. Although these materials can improve the photovoltaic performance, the increase in short-circuit current density (J SC) is limited, as their introduction can hinder charge transfer and reduce carrier collection efficiency [25,26]. Therefore, introducing a more effective skeleton structure is necessary to further enhance J SC and overall device performance.

In this study, we present a simple method to fabricate high-performance PSCs by incorpoarting 2D g-C3N4 nanosheets modified with 0D carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (g-C3N4/CQDs nanocomposite) into the perovskite precursor solution. CQDs exhibit unique photo-induced electron transfer and photoluminescence, which are excellent photoelectric materials. On one hand, incorporating g-C3N4/CQDs (CN/CQDs) can delay the crystallization process and increase the perovskite grain size, acting as skeleton structures that provide adequate growth space for the perovskite and the MA·CN/CQDs intermediate adduct. On the other hand, the coupled heterogeneous interface of CN/CQDs reduces the charge transfer barrier, enhancing J SC. The planar heterojunction MAPbI3-based PSCs achieved a maximum efficiency of 20.49 % and a higher J SC of 23.17 mA cm−2 with excellent stability.

Indium tin oxides (ITO) glass substrates, lead iodide (PbI2), methylammonium iodide (MAI), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, anhydrous, 99.9 %), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, anhydrous, 99.9 %), Spiro-OMeTAD, 4-tert-butylpyridine (tBP), Li-TFSI and chlorobenzene (CB) were purchased from Ying Kou You Xuan Trade Co., Ltd. SnO2 colloidal solution (12 % SnO2 in H2O colloidal dispersion) was purchased from Xi’an Yuri Solar Co., Ltd. Monohydrate citric acid (CA) was purchased from Sinopharm, and 1,2,4-benzeneetriamine dihydrochloride (BD) was purchased from Aladdin.

A specific amount of CA (1 mmol) and BD (4 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water to form a transparent solution. The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an electric oven under 200℃ for 24 h. The collected CQDs were dialyzed for 24 h in deionized water using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off. Finally, the water was removed through vacuum-rotary evaporation.

A one-pot thermal polymerization technique was used to prepare the g-C3N4/CQDs nanocomposite. A 10 mL 2 mg mL−1 CQDs solution was firstly added into 10 g of urea powder. The mixture precursor was placed in a covered alumina crucible and heated to 550°C in a muffle furnace at a rate of 5°C min−1. The temperature was maintained for 3 h. The collected powder is the as-prepared g-C3N4/CQDs.

The 80 μL of perovskite precursors (MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 or MAPbI3:g-C3N4/CQDs) were spin-coated onto cleaned FTO at 500 rpm for 3 s and 4000 rpm for 30 s. During the high-speed spinning process, 400 μL of chlorobenzene as antisolvent was dripped onto the perovskite film at 15 s before the end of the high- speed spinning program. The films on the substrates were then subjected to an annealing treatment at 100°C for 10 min in a glove box.
More details regarding the materials and characterization can be found in the supporting information.

Fig. 1a shows the experimental details and a schematic illustration of CN/CQDs. The structural formulas of g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs are provided in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). The as-synthesized CN/CQDs is a thin 2D/0D nanocomposite material. The CQDs were in-situ implanted onto the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets during the one-pot thermal polymerization process. Unlike normal physical adsorption, CQDs could provide numerous perfect sites on the g-C3N4 nanosheets through amide bonds, as confirmed by the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. S2). This bonding firmly anchors CQDs to the g-C3N4. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of as-prepared CN/CQDs composites exhibited a mass of highly porous grains (Fig. 1b). Figs. 1c and 1d show the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs of multi-hole g-C3N4 nanosheets and CQDs embedded in the g-C3N4 sheets. As shown in Fig. 1e, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image displays the formation of micro-regional heterostructures between the two materials with CQDs clearly distributed on the g-C3N4 nanosheets. The corresponding lattice spacing of 0.35 nm can be attributed to the (101) plane of carbon. This heterojunction structure is expected inhibit the carrier recombination and promote carrier transport as a functional material.

Fig. S3 displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, revealing the crystallization and phase structures of g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs nanocomposite. The diffraction peaks at 13.0° correspond to the in-plane tri-s-trizine units of g-C3N4, and the peaks observed around 27.5° correspond to the stacking of the conjugated aromatic structure, which are associated with g-C3N4 along c-axis. Upon the introduction of CQDs, both diffraction peaks broaden and decrease in intensity, indicating that the presence of CQDs hinders the thermopolymerization of tri-s-triazine units and the stacking and crystallization of g-C3N4 sheets. Furthermore, a closer examination of the (002) diffraction peak from g-C3N4 clearly reveals a shift toward a lower 2θ value after introduction of CQDs. Providing further evidence of crystal lattice distortion, which is consistent with previous reports. The Raman spectrum of CN/CQDs shows a stronger peak intensity and a blue shift compared to that of g-C3N4 (Fig. S4), aligning well with the XRD results. Fig. S5 shows the FTIR spectra of g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs. Both samples display a peak at 811 cm−1, which is ascribed to the typical bending vibration of tri-s-triazine units. The peaks between 900 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 correspond to the stretching modes of the CN heterocycles, while the broad band from 3000 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrational modes of amines. These results suggest that CQDs do not significantly affect the molecular structure of g-C3N4, which is consistent with previous reports [27,28].

The improvement in the crystallinity of perovskite films by introducing g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs was demonstrated by XRD measurements. The XRD patterns of MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films deposited on SnO2-coated FTO substrates are shown in Fig. 2a. All perovskite films exhibit the characteristic diffraction peaks at 14.1º, 28.3º and 31.8º, which can be assigned to (110), (220) and (310) planes of cubic perovskite structure. Notably, the XRD pattern of MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films significantly enhanced diffraction intensities, indicating improved crystal quality of the perovskite film with the incorporation of g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs. To further investigate the role of g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs in the crystallization of perovskite films, top-view SEM images of MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films are shown in Fig. 2b-d. While the surfaces of all perovskite films appear uniform, the control MAPbI3 film exhibits some voids and cracks, which would lead to poor charge transport and serious charge carrier recombination [29]. In contrast, the MAPbI3:CN/CQDs film demonstrates almost complete surface coverage, free of voids and cracks, with larger grains compared to the control film. The CN/CQDs on the surface of the perovskite film are not easily visible in SEM images due to their close proximity to the surface of perovskite grains. As illustrated in Fig. S6, the grain size distribution of MAPbI3 is dramatically increased by introducing g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs into the perovskite precursors. To further investigate the crystallization process, a serial of photographs of perovskite films during the initial annealing process (between 4 and 30 s) are shown in Fig. 2e. The slower color change observed in the MAPbI3:CN/CQDs film suggests that the introduction of CN/CQDs delays the growth process, which is beneficial for achieving films with larger crystal grain size [30]. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique was also used to characterize the interaction between perovskite with g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs. Fig. S7 shows the corresponding Pb 4 f XPS spectra of pristine MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4, and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films. Two main peaks located at 138 and 143 eV can be ascribed to the Pb 4 f7/2 and Pb 4 f5/2, respectively. Compared to the MAPbI3 film, both Pb 4 f7/2 and Pb 4 f5/2 peaks of MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs perovskite films shift to the higher binding energy. In contrast, such a phenomenon was almost negligible when we compared the MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films, which is ascribed to a passivation effect of g-C3N4.

To gain a deeper understanding of the mechanism for the increased grain size in MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films, the liquid-state 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) of perovskite precursors with and without CN/CQDs in deuterated N,N-dimethylformamide-d7 (DMF-d7) are shown as Fig. 3a. The liquid-state 1H NMR spectra were obtained by high-resolution liquid NMR spectrometer. For the measurement of liquid-state 1H NMR, the control sample of perovskite precursor solution was prepared by dissolving MAI:PbI2 (1.2 mol:1.2 mol) in 1 mL of DMF-d7. A certain amount of CN/CQDs (0.3 mg mL−1) was added to the precursor solution as the modified sample. DMF-d7 was chosen as the solvent to avoid effect from other solvents in the spectra. Besides the 1H NMR peaks from DMF-d7 marked by stars in Fig. 3a, the broad peak at δ= 7.51 ppm is associated with four hydrogen atoms tightly attached to adjacent nitrogen atoms of the MA+, which appeared a clear shift to high value via incorporation of CN/CQDs in the perovskite precursors. Fig. 3b displays the high-magnification spectrum between 7.3 and 7.8 ppm. Normally, the tri-s-triazine rings in g-C3N4 exhibit strong Lewis basicity, creating a Lewis basic environment around MA+. Therefore, the observed shift in the peak is primarily due to the interaction between g-C3N4 and MA+ based on the theory of Lewis. This interaction suggests the formation of an intermediate adduct of MA·CN/CQDs, which serves as heterogeneous nucleation sites for the growth of MAPbI3 crystal grains.

Based on these analyses, we propose a possible model for improved growth of the MAPbI3 film upon the introducing of CN/CQDs, as illustrated schematically in Fig. 3c. The g-C3N4 acts as a type of skeleton structure with its planar 2D lamellar structure, which provides ample growth space for the crystallization of MAPbI3. In step 1, intermediate adducts of MA·CN/CQDs (yellow ball in step 1) are formed to provide more effective nucleation sites for crystallization of perovskite phase and slow down the crystallization. In step 2, the bonding between PbI2 and MAI is stronger than that between CN/CQDs and MAI, which leads to PbI2 reacting with the intermediate adduct of MA·CN/CQDs, and replacing CN/CQDs during the annealing process, resulting in the formation of MAPbI3 (yellow balls in step 2). In step 3, the MAPbI3 crystals grow into large grains within the available space. The presence of CN/CQDs within the grains effectively retards the electron-hole recombination. The CQDs, with their sp2-hybridized nitrogen atoms, act as an electron buffer, facilitating the transfer of exited electron and p-electron delocalization in the micro-region, further suppressing recombination. During the synthesis of CN/CQDs, the micro-regional heterostructure generates a work function difference between the CQDs and the conduction band of g-C3N4, providing enhanced photocarrier transfer kinetics and promoting electron-hole pair separation.

We prepared n-i-p planar devices with a stack of FTO/SnO2/MAPbI3:CN/CQDs /Spiro-OMeTAD/Ag to further investigate the impact of CN/CQDs on the photovoltaic performance of PSCs, as shown in Fig. 4a. Figs. 4b and 4c show the cross-sectional SEM images of the PSCs based on MAPbI3 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films. Obviously, the thickness of MAPbI3 is only 305 nm, while the MAPbI3:CN/CQDs film is slightly thicker at 360 nm, which is still thinner than the typical 400 nm. The addition of the skeleton structure contributes to the increased film thickness, which enhances the light absorption capacity [31]. In Fig. 4d, the UV–vis absorption spectra of control, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films exhibit the typical absorption of perovskite. Compared to the control film, both the MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films exhibit a noticeable increase in light absorption intensity, primarily due to the thicker thickness and improved crystal quality. This enhancement in light absorption directly translates into better light-harvesting capability for the devices [32]. The bandgap diagram of the device is shown in Fig. 4e.

Fig. 5a shows the photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) characteristics of PSCs based on MAPbI3 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs. The control device achieves a PCE of 17.94 % with an open circuit voltage (V OC) of 1.08 V, a J SC of 21.65 mA cm−2 and a fill factor (FF) of 77.29 %. As expected, the CN/CQDs based PSCs exhibit the highest PCE of 20.49 % with a V OC of 1.13 V, a significantly improved J SC of 23.17 mA cm−2 and an FF of 78.37 %. It is worth noting that the enhanced PCE in PSCs with CN/CQDs is mainly due to the improved FF, which is ascribed to the passivation effect of g-C3N4 with better interface contact. The improvement in FF is due to the larger grains, which reduce the number of grain boundaries and facilitate charge transport and collection. The improvement in J SC is linked to the enhanced light-harvesting ability of the optimized perovskite film, along with the reduction of smaller grains atop the larger grains. Compared to the pristine perovskite film, the better light-harvesting properties of the perovskite films based on CN/CQDs are further confirmed by the enhanced absorption. Meanwhile, the CN/CQDs could simultaneously facilitate photoelectron transport and suppress charge recombination through their specially coupled heterogeneous interface, which also is the reason for improvement of J SC and PCE. Table 1 summarizes the impact of CN/CQDs concentration in the perovskite precursor on the electrical performance of the device, in which higher concentration of CN/CQDs would lead to an increase number of nucleation sites.

As shown in Fig. S8 and Table S1, there is negligible discrepancy between the reverse and forward scans of the PSCs based on MAPbI3:CN/CQDs. The plot of the external quantum efficiency (EQE) is illustrated in Fig. 5b. Obviously, the light-harvesting properties of MAPbI3:CN/CQDs are better than pristine perovskite and MAPbI3:g-C3N4. The integrated J SC values calculated from EQE spectra are 20.23 mA cm−2, 21.10 mA cm−2 and 22.15 mA cm−2 for devices based on MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs, respectively. Fig. S9 shows the statistical histograms chart of PCE over 30 control and CN/CQDs-based PSCs, showing clear enhancement in PCE for the CN/CQDs-based PSCs compared to the control device, indicating favorable reproducibility of photovoltaic performance [33]. Such variation tendency of photovoltaic performance is obviously consistent with enlarged grains and improved crystal quality of perovskite films caused by introducing CN/CQDs. To further explore the underlying mechanism of improved photovoltaic performance of PSCs, we analyzed the photovoltaic parameters in detail. Fig. S10 shows the corresponding average photovoltaic parameters for various batches of PSCs based on pristine MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films. Obviously, the average PCE of PSCs based on pristine MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs exhibits a gradual increase. Compared to the control devices, both MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs only show slight increase in V OC and FF, but their J SC boosted greatly. The increase in J SC for MAPbI3:CN/CQDs-based PSCs is much greater than that of MAPbI3:g-C3N4-based PSCs. Since the crystal quality and surface morphology of both perovskite films are quite similar, we speculate that such variation in J SC may be due to the different charge transfer within perovskite absorption layer [34–36]. Table 2 summarizes the cases of carbon nitride compounds in PSCs reported previously. It is observed that our current work achieves the highest PCE, demonstrating that this approach is a promising strategy for enhancing photovoltaic performance of devices. The stability of PSCs is a major challenge in large-scale PSC production. To explore the effect of CN/CQDs on the stability of PSCs, the steady-state photocurrent and efficiency were tested at the maximum power state (0.971 V) under one-sun illumination for 250 s, as shown in Fig. 5c. This indicates the introduction of CN/CQDs does not compromise irradiation stability of the devices.

To investigate charge transfer, the photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved PL (TRPL) spectra of MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs on FTO/SnO2 substrates are shown in Figs. 6a and 6b. The PL intensity of MAPbI3:CN/CQDs film significantly quenched compared to the pristine MAPbI3 film, suggesting improved charge extraction and transfer [37]. The corresponding TRPL decay curves are presented in Fig. 6b. The carrier lifetimes of both samples were extracted by fitting curves with following biexponential decay function:
(1)
y fit = y 0 + A 1 exp(-t/τ1) + A 2 exp(-t/τ2)

where y 0 is a constant for the baseline offset. The fast decay constant (τ1) expresses efficiency of charge extraction, and the slow decay time (τ2) is responsible for radiative recombination. A 1 and A 2 are the amplitudes [38]. Table S2 shows the fitting data of those TRPL spectra. Compared to the MAPbI3 film τ1= 164.32 ns and τ2= 76.91 ns, and the MAPbI3:g-C3N4 film with τ1= 107.63 ns and τ2= 95.88 ns, the MAPbI3:CN/CQDs film exhibits lifetime constants of τ1= 53.28 ns and τ2= 109.54 ns. The decreased τ1 and increased τ2 manifest that the introduction of CN/CQDs accelerates the separation of charge carriers and effectively inhibits non-radiative recombination, resulting in an enhanced J SC [39].

We further investigated the charge carrier transport and recombination properties of PSCs by electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. 6c shows the fitted electrical equivalent circuit model and the Nyquist plots of devices based on MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs. The measurements were conducted under dark conditions with an applied voltage of 0.8 V. The equivalent circuit composed of series resistance (Rs), recombination resistance (Rrec) and a parallel capacitor was employed to extract the values of Rs and Rrec, where Rs represents the series resistance, which is reflected in the high-frequency region and obtained from the intercept in the x-axis of the high-frequency curve, Rrec represents recombination resistance, which was reflected in the low-frequency region [45]. While Rs was assigned to the high-frequency region (>500 Hz) in EIS, Rrec (<500 Hz) corresponds to the low-frequency region. The equivalent circuit curve fitting method was utilized to obtain the fitting parameters, as summarized in Table S3. The PSCs based on MAPbI3:CN/CQDs exhibited lower Rs and higher Rrec, indicating better contact properties at the hetero-interface and reduced carrier recombination, which enhances charge transport. These results are consistent with the results of PL and TRPL [46,47].

The improvement of charge transfer performance is directly related to the density of defect states in the device. So, we fabricate the electron-only devices with the structure of FTO/SnO2/perovskite/PCBM/Ag to obtain the trap density variations of MAPbI3, MAPbI3:g-C3N4 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs films. Fig. 6d shows the dark J-V characteristics curves, which is quantitatively measured by the trap-filling limit voltage (V TFL) obtained by space charge limited current (SCLC). The trap state density can be calculated using the following equation [48],
(2)
N
t
=
2 ε 0 ε
V
TFL
q
L
2
where ɛ
0 is the permittivity of vacuum, ɛ is the relative dielectric constant of MAPbI3, q is the electronic charge, L is the thickness of the MAPbI3 layer, and V TFL is the onset voltage of the trap-filled limit region. The calculated trap density decreases from 2.23 × 1016 cm−3 in the pristine MAPbI3-based device to 1.91 × 1016 cm−3 in MAPbI3:g-C3N4-based device and 1.88 × 1016 cm−3 in MAPbI3:CN/CQDs-based device. The reduced trap density suggests that the introduction of CN/CQDs passivate defects in perovskite film, which contributes to enhanced crystal quality and improved J SC [49].

As we know, the charge behavior and photovoltaic performance of PSCs are primarily determined by the perovskite absorption layer. The XRD results showed that the crystallization quality of MAPbI3:CN/CQDs was similar with that of MAPbI3:g-C3N4. To further understand the enhanced charge transfer in MAPbI3:CN/CQDs-based PSCs, we focused on the microstructure of CN/CQDs within the perovskite films. XPS was employed to investigate the chemical states and interaction between g-C3N4 and CQDs. Fig. 7a shows the survey XPS spectra of g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs, indicating the coexistence of C, N and O elements. As shown in the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra (Fig. 7b), two distinct peaks at 284.6 eV and 288.1 eV corresponds to carbon in CC and sp2-hybridized carbon (N–CN) in g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs, respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra in Fig. 7c show the peaks located at 399.1 eV, 400.4 eV and 401.5 eV, which are attributed to sp2-hybridized nitrogen in tri-s-triazine rings (CN–C, referred to as X1), bridging nitrogen ((C)3-N, referred to as X2) and amino groups (N-H, referred to as X3), respectively. It is noteworthy that peak intensity ratio of X1/(X2+X3) for g-C3N4 (4.21) in Fig. 7d is lower than that of CN/CQDs (5.59), indicating that CN/CQDs contain more CN–C groups. The increase in the coalescence of amide bonds enhances the number of coupled heterogeneous domains, which can promote charge transport and reduce non-radiative recombination [50].

We then utilized density functional theory (DFT) calculation to evaluate the band structure of the micro-regional CN/CQDs heterostructures. The optimized structures of g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs are shown in Fig. S11a and S11b, respectively. Fig. S11c and S11d displays the electron density of states (DOS) for g-C3N4 and CN/CQDs. Both systems have their Fermi levels positioned in the middle of the energy band gap. Obviously, the g-C3N4 exhibits a character of semiconductor. But a mass of electron density is distributed around the Fermi level for CN/CQDs. Thus, the introducing of CN/CQDs heterostructure is beneficial for facilitating charge extraction and transport effectively [51].

The long-term stability of PSCs with and without CN/CQDs was conducted in Fig. 8, which are stored under ambient environment. Fig. 8a shows the normalized PCE over time. The PCE of the device based on CN/CQDs maintains more than 90 % of its initial value after 500 h, while the MAPbI3:CN/CQDs device drops to 71 % of its original efficiency. The reason for enhancement of the long-term stability is mainly attributed to the enhanced crystallization of the perovskite film by introducing CN/CQDs, which improves the crystal quality. Subsequently, the thermal stability of devices based on MAPbI3 and MAPbI3:CN/CQDs was also explored. Fig. 8b shows the efficiency values of both devices after annealing at different temperatures for 40 min. As the temperature increases to 160°C, the PCE of the PSC based on MAPbI3:CN/CQDs remains above 80 %, while the PCE of the PSC based on MAPbI3 reduces to 30 % of its initial efficiency. Fig. 8c shows the efficiency versus the annealing time (temperature:160°C) for both samples. The normalized PCE of the control device gradually decreases to less than 20 % after 50 min. The devices optimized with CN/CQDs still maintained about 80 %, with significantly improved thermal stability. It is well known that thermal degradation of perovskite films typically occurs at the grain boundaries because of ion migration and escape from the crystal lattice. The incorporating CN/CQDs can delay the crystallization process, increase the perovskite grain sizes and reduce grain boundaries. As a result, the PSCs based on MAPbI3:CN/CQDs exhibit enhanced environmental stability [52].

The potential for CN/CQDs in the commercialization of PSCs lies in its unique properties, which can address key challenges in the field. Both g-C3N4 and CQDs are derived from abundant and inexpensive elements, making them cost-effective choices for large-scale applications. Meanwhile, they can be synthesized using straightforward and scalable methods, such as thermal polymerization or solution-based processes, which are compatible with industrial-scale production. Incorporating g-C3N4 and CQDs enhances the moisture, thermal, and photostability of perovskite layers, which are critical for improving the operational lifetime of PSCs and addressing the challenges related to commercialization. Based on the analysis above, CN/CQDs have the potential to mitigate degradation pathways, such as ion migration and lead leakage, thereby enhancing the operational lifetime of PSCs to meet industrial standards. As green materials, CN/CQDs also offer additional benefits due to their compatibility with lead-free perovskite systems, which further reduces environmental risks. The successful commercialization of CN/CQDs-enhanced PSCs will rely on their compatibility with existing production systems. With minimal modifications to current manufacturing processes, the widespread adoption of these devices could be significantly accelerated.

In summary, we propose a novel strategy to slow down MAPbI3 crystallization via incorporating g-C3N4/CQDs into the perovskite absorber to obtain thin perovskite films. The CN/CQDs skeleton model provides sufficient space for the growth of perovskite grains, facilitating the enhancement of light-harvesting capacity. Furthermore, the specially coupled heterogeneous interface of CN/CQDs effectively suppressed charge-carrier recombination at the surface and greatly improved the J SC. In consequence, the PCE of 20.49 % has been achieved from MAPbI3:CN/CQDs- based PSCs. This study provides valuable insights into the role of 2D/0D heterostructures in boosting the photovoltaic performance of PSCs, and encourages further exploration into the design of new microscopic heterojunctions for advancing the development of PSC technology.

Gao Fei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Investigation. Shao Long: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Li Ye: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Chen Weijun: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. He Li: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Postdoctoral Fellowship Program of China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant Number GZC20232238), the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. U2141239), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 62305045).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2025.179119.
